scenario: An Inclusive Gateway has 4 outgoing flows: =score > 90, =score > 70 and customerLoyalty = "GOLD", =specialOffer != null, and a default flow. The team is unclear on when the default flow is taken -- only if no other condition matches, or in some other scenario?
question: Which best describes Inclusive Gateway default flow behaviour?

options.text: The default flow on an Inclusive Gateway is always taken in addition to any conditional flows that evaluate to true -- it fires alongside matching paths as an always-on extra branch -- this description does not reflect the actual behaviour. The default flow is a fallback mechanism, not an always-active parallel path. It fires only when no other outgoing conditional sequence flow evaluates to true. When at least one conditional path matches and activates, the default flow is suppressed entirely. Treating it as always-active would double the downstream work by adding the default path to every successful condition match.

Default flow is taken only if no other outgoing condition evaluates to true. The engine evaluates all conditioned outgoing flows first; if any evaluate to true, those branches activate and the default is not taken. Only when every conditional flow evaluates to false does the default path activate as a fallback. This behaviour ensures the gateway always produces at least one outgoing token, preventing deadlock when no conditions match. md, the default flow is marked with a small diagonal slash on the sequence flow arrow and requires no condition expression on the sequence flow element itself.

The default flow on an Inclusive Gateway fires when exactly one conditional outgoing sequence flow evaluates to true, serving as an indicator that only a single branch was activated -- this description does not reflect the actual behaviour. The number of matching conditional flows, whether one, two, three, or all, has no bearing on whether the default fires. The default fires only when zero conditional flows evaluate to true. Its sole purpose is preventing a gateway deadlock when all conditions are false. The match count is irrelevant to whether the default path activates.

Default flows are not permitted on Inclusive Gateways -- only Exclusive Gateways support a default flow in the BPMN specification -- this description does not reflect the actual behaviour. Both Exclusive and Inclusive Gateways support default flows per the BPMN specification. On an Inclusive Gateway, the default flow provides the same safety net as on an Exclusive Gateway: it fires when no conditional path evaluates to true, preventing the gateway from deadlocking with no active outgoing path. Parallel Gateways do not use default flows because all outgoing paths always fire regardless of conditions.

a.text: Incorrect. Default flow semantic on conditional gateways (Exclusive and Inclusive): "take this flow if no other outgoing flow's condition matched." For Inclusive, this means: evaluate all 3 conditioned flows; if at least one is true, activate those; default is not taken. If all 3 are false, default is taken. The default ensures the gateway always emits at least one token, preventing deadlock when no condition matches. Modelling note: the default flow doesn't have a condition expression (it's "no condition"); some modelers indicate it visually with a diagonal slash on the arrow.

b.text: Correct. Default is fallback, not always-also.

c.text: Incorrect. Defaults don't track match count.

d.text: Incorrect. Defaults are valid on Inclusive.
